answers.answer: The SSE endpoints mentioned in the question do NOT exist. There are no /v1/markets/{id}/stream or /v1/accounts/{id}/risk/stream endpoints in the backend. The backend uses Python's http.server.BaseHTTPRequestHandler with no SSE infrastructure. However, the frontend ALREADY has a complete WebSocket client: useMarketPriceSubscription() in hooks.ts:132 connects to /ws/markets/{marketId}/prices via the browser WebSocket API, with exponential backoff reconnection (500ms-5000ms), sequence tracking, and React Query cache integration. The task specifically requires WebSocket because (a) the task title says 'WebSocket', (b) the frontend WebSocket client is already built and wired into MarketDetail.tsx:26, and (c) SSE would require rewriting the existing frontend client. The work is adding a WebSocket handler to the Python backend to serve the already-built frontend client.
answers.uncertainty: None - the codebase state is unambiguous. SSE doesn't exist, WebSocket frontend client does exist, backend handler is missing.

answers.answer: The frontend's MarketPriceMessage type (types.ts:40-54) defines exactly what the WebSocket should send: { marketId, status, marginals, seq, emittedAt, approxFlag, resolution?, resolutionProbabilities?, terminalEvent? }. The parseMarketPriceMessage validator (hooks.ts:90-130) enforces this shape. The hook updates React Query cache with status + marginals + resolution + resolutionProbabilities on each message (hooks.ts:220-228), then invalidates dependent queries (events, engine-stats, market list). When status !== 'active', it treats the market as terminal and closes the socket (hooks.ts:234-238). So the backend WebSocket should broadcast a MarketPriceMessage whenever: (1) marginals change after a probability edit or trade, (2) market is resolved/closed. The event journal events (CommandAccepted/CommandRejected via emit_terminal_event at server.py:3135) provide the seq numbers and should trigger broadcasts.
answers.uncertainty: None - the MarketPriceMessage contract is well-defined in the frontend.

answers.answer: The frontend already implements a hybrid approach: React Query polling at 5000ms (hooks.ts:277,285) AND the WebSocket subscription. The WebSocket hook supplements polling rather than replacing it. On WebSocket reconnect, it refetches via React Query (hooks.ts:176,193). On close, it falls back to polling (hooks.ts:255-256). React Query is the source of truth (setQueryData updates the cache, hooks.ts:215). The architecture is already designed for coexistence - no changes needed to the polling strategy. Optionally, polling interval could be relaxed to 30s when WebSocket is connected, but this is a future optimization, not required for initial implementation.
answers.uncertainty: None - the dual strategy is already implemented in the frontend.

answers.answer: ProbabilityBar already has CSS transition on probability bar segment widths: transition: 'width 0.3s ease' (ProbabilityBar.tsx:34). VariableRow also has transition: 'width 0.2s ease' (line 98 in assumptions). This means when marginals update via WebSocket, the probability bars will smoothly animate to their new widths automatically. No additional flash/highlight animation infrastructure exists (no framer-motion, react-spring - all animations are inline CSS transitions). For the initial implementation, the existing smooth width transitions provide sufficient visual feedback. Flash animations (e.g., background color flash on change) could be added later but aren't needed now.
answers.uncertainty: User may want more noticeable visual feedback (flash/highlight) beyond smooth width transitions, but existing transitions provide baseline UX.

answers.answer: MarketDetail is the only view that needs WebSocket and it's ALREADY wired up. MarketDetail.tsx:26-28 calls useMarketPriceSubscription(marketId, { enabled: isActiveMarket }). MarketList (MarketList.tsx) uses useMarkets() with 5000ms polling - subscribing to WebSocket for every market in the list would require many concurrent connections, which is impractical. Portfolio (Portfolio.tsx) uses useMarkets() + useAccountExposure() + useAccountRisk(), all with polling. System (System.tsx) uses useHealth() + useServiceIndex() + useMarkets(). No other views use useMarketPriceSubscription. The initial scope is correctly MarketDetail only - other views can continue with polling.
answers.uncertainty: None - only MarketDetail uses WebSocket subscription.

assumption_checks.impact: SSE endpoints do NOT exist in the backend. The frontend is built for WebSocket (useMarketPriceSubscription uses browser WebSocket API connecting to /ws/markets/{id}/prices). The task requires adding a WebSocket handler to the backend, not using SSE. This is a backend-focused task, not frontend-only.

The hook already exists but uses WebSocket, not EventSource. useMarketPriceSubscription (hooks.ts:132) is a complete WebSocket client with reconnection, sequence tracking, and React Query cache integration via setQueryData. No new frontend hook is needed - only the backend WebSocket handler.
MarketDetail is the only view using useMarketPriceSubscription (MarketDetail.tsx:26). Other views use polling. Scope is confirmed to be MarketDetail only.

Not SSE/EventSource - the frontend uses WebSocket with custom exponential backoff reconnection (hooks.ts:164-179). fromSeq is for REST event pagination (server.py:2110), not WebSocket replay. The reconnection logic is already implemented in the frontend and works by refetching via React Query on reconnect. No changes needed to reconnection strategy.

The frontend already implements dual-path: polling at 5000ms + WebSocket. React Query is the source of truth. WebSocket updates feed into the cache via setQueryData. Polling continues as fallback. No changes needed to this architecture.

decomposition_assessment.decision_reason: All questions answered with confidence >= 0.90. The codebase situation is clear: the frontend WebSocket client is complete and wired in, and only the backend WebSocket handler needs to be implemented. Key technical challenge is that the backend uses Python's http.server.HTTPServer which doesn't natively support WebSocket upgrades - will need to either implement WebSocket upgrade handshake manually in BayesHandler or use a library like wsproto/websockets. This is a focused backend task (~1 file, ~100-200 lines) with a well-defined contract (MarketPriceMessage format). No children needed.
